messages.content:
What does it do, what technologies and concepts are used and how does it reach its goal. Step by step by step, divide these contents into smaller parts and explain.A fast TCP/UDP tunnel over HTTP

Chisel is a fast TCP/UDP tunnel, transported over HTTP, secured via SSH. Single executable including both client and server. Written in Go (golang). Chisel is mainly useful for passing through firewalls, though it can also be used to provide a secure endpoint into your network.

Easy to use
Performant*
Encrypted connections using the SSH protocol (via crypto/ssh)
Authenticated connections; authenticated client connections with a users config file, authenticated server connections with fingerprint matching.
Client auto-reconnects with exponential backoff
Clients can create multiple tunnel endpoints over one TCP connection
Clients can optionally pass through SOCKS or HTTP CONNECT proxies
Reverse port forwarding (Connections go through the server and out the client)
Server optionally doubles as a reverse proxy
Server optionally allows SOCKS5 connections (See guide below)
Clients optionally allow SOCKS5 connections from a reversed port forward
Client connections over stdio which supports ssh -o ProxyCommand providing SSH over HTTP

$ chisel --help

  Usage: chisel [command] [--help]

  Version: X.Y.Z

  Commands:
    server - runs chisel in server mode
    client - runs chisel in client mode

  Read more:
    https://github.com/jpillora/chisel
$ chisel server --help

  Usage: chisel server [options]

  Options:

    --host, Defines the HTTP listening host – the network interface
    (defaults the environment variable HOST and falls back to 0.0.0.0).

    --port, -p, Defines the HTTP listening port (defaults to the environment
    variable PORT and fallsback to port 8080).

    --key, (deprecated use --keygen and --keyfile instead)
    An optional string to seed the generation of a ECDSA public
    and private key pair. All communications will be secured using this
    key pair. Share the subsequent fingerprint with clients to enable detection
    of man-in-the-middle attacks (defaults to the CHISEL_KEY environment
    variable, otherwise a new key is generate each run).

    --keygen, A path to write a newly generated PEM-encoded SSH private key file.
    If users depend on your --key fingerprint, you may also include your --key to
    output your existing key. Use - (dash) to output the generated key to stdout.

    --keyfile, An optional path to a PEM-encoded SSH private key. When
    this flag is set, the --key option is ignored, and the provided private key
    is used to secure all communications. (defaults to the CHISEL_KEY_FILE
    environment variable). Since ECDSA keys are short, you may also set keyfile
    to an inline base64 private key (e.g. chisel server --keygen - | base64).

    --authfile, An optional path to a users.json file. This file should
    be an object with users defined like:
      {
        "<user:pass>": ["<addr-regex>","<addr-regex>"]
      }
    when <user> connects, their <pass> will be verified and then
    each of the remote addresses will be compared against the list
    of address regular expressions for a match. Addresses will
    always come in the form "<remote-host>:<remote-port>" for normal remotes
    and "R:<local-interface>:<local-port>" for reverse port forwarding
    remotes. This file will be automatically reloaded on change.

    --auth, An optional string representing a single user with full
    access, in the form of <user:pass>. It is equivalent to creating an
    authfile with {"<user:pass>": [""]}. If unset, it will use the
    environment variable AUTH.

    --keepalive, An optional keepalive interval. Since the underlying
    transport is HTTP, in many instances we'll be traversing through
    proxies, often these proxies will close idle connections. You must
    specify a time with a unit, for example '5s' or '2m'. Defaults
    to '25s' (set to 0s to disable).

    --backend, Specifies another HTTP server to proxy requests to when
    chisel receives a normal HTTP request. Useful for hiding chisel in
    plain sight.

    --socks5, Allow clients to access the internal SOCKS5 proxy. See
    chisel client --help for more information.

    --reverse, Allow clients to specify reverse port forwarding remotes
    in addition to normal remotes.

    --tls-key, Enables TLS and provides optional path to a PEM-encoded
    TLS private key. When this flag is set, you must also set --tls-cert,
    and you cannot set --tls-domain.

    --tls-cert, Enables TLS and provides optional path to a PEM-encoded
    TLS certificate. When this flag is set, you must also set --tls-key,
    and you cannot set --tls-domain.

    --tls-domain, Enables TLS and automatically acquires a TLS key and
    certificate using LetsEncrypt. Setting --tls-domain requires port 443.
    You may specify multiple --tls-domain flags to serve multiple domains.
    The resulting files are cached in the "$HOME/.cache/chisel" directory.
    You can modify this path by setting the CHISEL_LE_CACHE variable,
    or disable caching by setting this variable to "-". You can optionally
    provide a certificate notification email by setting CHISEL_LE_EMAIL.

    --tls-ca, a path to a PEM encoded CA certificate bundle or a directory
    holding multiple PEM encode CA certificate bundle files, which is used to 
    validate client connections. The provided CA certificates will be used 
    instead of the system roots. This is commonly used to implement mutual-TLS. 

    --pid Generate pid file in current working directory

    -v, Enable verbose logging

    --help, This help text

  Signals:
    The chisel process is listening for:
      a SIGUSR2 to print process stats, and
      a SIGHUP to short-circuit the client reconnect timer

  Version:
    X.Y.Z

  Read more:
    https://github.com/jpillora/chisel

Chisel is an open-source tool used to create a secure communication tunnel over HTTP by leveraging existing SSH protocols for encryption and authentication. Below I’ll explain the concepts and technologies involved in setting up and using such a tunnel.

**Key Concepts and Technologies:**

1. **TCP & UDP:**
   - TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) and UDP (User Datagram Protocol) are two core protocols of the Internet protocol suite.
   - TCP is connection-oriented and ensures the delivery of data packets in the same order they were sent, suited for reliable communication.
   - UDP is connectionless and does not guarantee order or delivery, suited for fast and real-time communication scenarios.

2. **HTTP Tunneling:**
   - HTTP tunneling is a technique by which non-HTTP traffic can be encapsulated within HTTP requests and responses.
   - This allows data to pass through firewalls and proxies that allow only HTTP traffic.

3. **SSH (Secure Shell):**
   - SSH is a cryptographic network protocol for secure data communication, remote command-line login, remote command execution, and other secure network services between two networked computers.
   - SSH operates on a client-server model and uses public-key cryptography to authenticate the remote computer and allow the remote computer to authenticate the user, if necessary.

4. **Go (Golang):**
   - Go, also known as Golang, is an open-source programming language that makes it easy to build simple, reliable, and efficient software.
   - Chisel is written in Go, likely for its performance capabilities and ease of cross-compiling to various platforms.

5. **SOCKS Proxy:**
   - SOCKS is an Internet protocol that routes network packets between a client and server through a proxy server.
   - Chisel allows tunneling SOCKS over the HTTP connection, which can be useful for passing all your traffic through the SSH tunnel.

6. **Port Forwarding (Including Reverse Port Forwarding):**
   - Port forwarding allows remote computers to connect to a specific computer or service within a private local-area network (LAN).
   - Reverse port forwarding is the opposite, whereby connections from the server side can be redirected to the client, which is behind a firewall or NAT.

**How Chisel Works (Step by Step):**

1. **Setup Server:**
   - You run Chisel in server mode on a machine that is accessible from the internet. The server listens on an HTTP port for incoming client connections.
   - The Chisel server can be configured with authentication and encryption parameters, like a list of authenticated users and an SSH key pair.

2. **Setup Client:**
   - On the client computer, which is often behind a firewall or NAT, you run Chisel in client mode.
   - You configure the client to establish a connection to the server over HTTP (which means it can pass through firewalls if allowed).
   - The client authenticates with the server using specified credentials (SSH user/password or keys).

3. **Tunnel Establishment:**
   - Once the client connects to the Chisel server, a secure SSH tunnel is established over the HTTP connection.
   - Encrypted data can now flow through the tunnel, protected from eavesdropping or tampering.

4. **Port Forwarding:**
   - You can configure port forwarding rules to direct traffic from specific local ports to remote hosts and ports through the tunnel.
   - Reverse port forwarding is also possible, where a remote server can access ports on the client machine.

5. **Data Transmission:**
   - Data is transmitted through the established tunnel using the configured TCP/UDP ports.
   - The encryption and integrity of the transmission are handled by the underlying SSH protocol, ensuring secure communication.

6. **Manage Connectivity:**
   - Chisel includes auto-reconnect with exponential backoff, in case the connection drops.
   - The server can handle multiple clients, each with their own tunnel configuration.

7. **Utilization of the Tunnel:**
   - Applications on the client machine can use the local ports set up for forwarding as if they were connecting directly to the remote service.
   - The applications don't need to be aware that their data is being sent through an HTTP tunnel over SSH—they just make normal TCP/UDP connections.

This system is useful in situations where you need to bypass strict firewalls, or when you need to secure your network communications over an untrusted network. Chisel's functionality can be controlled via command-line flags as described in the help output, and it can be further customized with config files and environment variables for automated setups or more complex scenarios.

so then how this work? ### Server Setup:

1. Check Open Ports on Your Server:

Connect to your server via SSH and check which ports are open and listening. Ensure port 80 is not being used by another service:

   ssh <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>
   netstat -tulnp

   If another service is using port 80, consider changing its configuration to free up the port.

2. Install Chisel on Your Server:

   Log in to your server and use the following commands to download and set up Chisel:

   mkdir chisel && cd chisel
   wget https://github.com/jpillora/chisel/releases/download/v1.9.1/chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64.gz
   gunzip chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64.gz
   chmod +x chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64


3. Run Chisel in a Persistent Session:

   We will use tmux, a terminal multiplexer, so our Chisel server keeps running even if we disconnect from SSH:

   tmux new -s chisel_session
   ./chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64 server --port 80 --socks5 --proxy http://your-subdomain.yourdomain.com -v

   To detach from the tmux session and leave it running in the background, press Ctrl+B followed by D.

### Client Setup (Windows):

1. Download Chisel Windows Client:
   
   On your Windows machine, download the Chisel client from the GitHub releases page (make sure to choose the correct version for Windows).

2. Setup Chisel Client:

   Extract the downloaded .gz file. To connect to your Chisel server, open Command Prompt in the directory where you’ve extracted Chisel and execute:

   chisel.exe client http://your-subdomain.yourdomain.com 127.0.0.1:5050:socks


### Client Setup (Android):

1. Install Termux and Go Language:

   Download and install Termux from the Play Store. Open Termux and install Go by running the following commands:

   pkg update
   pkg install wget
   pkg install golang


2. Download and Prepare Chisel on Android:

   In Termux, create a new directory for Chisel, download it, and give execution permissions:

   mkdir chisel && cd chisel
   wget https://github.com/jpillora/chisel/releases/download/v1.9.1/chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64.gz
   gunzip chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64.gz
   chmod +x chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64


3. Connect to Chisel Server:

   Run the following command in Termux:

   ./chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64 client http://your-subdomain.yourdomain.com 5050:127.0.0.1:443


https://github.com/jpillora/chisel

The steps you've outlined show how to set up Chisel to create a secure tunnel from both Windows and Android clients to a central Chisel server. Let's go through the steps for each setup to understand how it works.

### Server Setup:

**1. Check Open Ports on Your Server:**

You first need to ensure that the port you intend to use for the Chisel server isn't already occupied by another service. Port 80 is commonly used for regular HTTP traffic, so it's important to ensure it's available for Chisel unless you plan to use another port. You do this by connecting to your server via SSH and listing the open ports. 

**2. Install Chisel on Your Server:**

The next step is to download and install the Chisel server executable on your server. You are downloading a specific version of Chisel from the GitHub releases page, extracting it, and making it executable with `chmod`.

**3. Run Chisel in a Persistent Session:**

Using `tmux`, you start a new session to run the Chisel server. The `--port 80` flag indicates that Chisel is listening on port 80, `--socks5` enables a SOCKS5 proxy on the server, and `--proxy` sets up a reverse proxy to your subdomain. `-v` enables verbose logging to help with troubleshooting.

The `tmux` session persists after you've logged out, ensuring the Chisel server keeps running.

### Client Setup (Windows):

**1. Download Chisel Windows Client:**

You download the correct Chisel client for Windows from the releases page on GitHub.

**2. Setup Chisel Client:**

In a command prompt, you run the Chisel client connecting to your server's domain. `127.0.0.1:5050:socks` tells Chisel to forward traffic from the Windows machine's local port 5050 to the SOCKS5 proxy on the Chisel server.

### Client Setup (Android):

**1. Install Termux and Go Language:**

Termux is a terminal emulator for Android. After installing Termux, Go language is installed via its package manager (`pkg`). This might not be necessary for running Chisel, but the instructions include it.

**2. Download and Prepare Chisel on Android:**

Similar to the server setup, you download and prepare the Chisel client for Android, ensuring you grab the version suited for the ARM architecture of Android devices.

**3. Connect to Chisel Server:**

You run the Chisel client within Termux to connect to your Chisel server's domain. `5050:127.0.0.1:443` tells Chisel to forward traffic from the server port 5050 to the local port 443 on the Android device, effectively forwarding traffic through the tunnel to the Android device's HTTPS port (443).

What you're setting up is a system in which devices in different locations and behind various firewalls or NATs are able to establish secure, authenticated, and encrypted tunnels to a central server. This central server can take requests from these remote clients and forward the traffic either locally or to other destinations, while the clients can also expose local ports to be accessible through the server. This enables secure communication for situations where direct connections might be blocked or insecure.
so i have a socks proxy or open vpn or wirguad proxy setup on my server , is it possible that i incorporated them in to Chisel  tunnel ?

Yes, Chisel can be used to encapsulate other proxy connections, like SOCKS, OpenVPN, or WireGuard, within its tunnel. This is especially useful if you need to bypass restrictive firewalls or if you're in a network environment that blocks standard VPN protocols but allows HTTP/HTTPS traffic. Here’s how you could incorporate them into a Chisel tunnel:

### SOCKS Proxy Through Chisel

If you already have a SOCKS proxy server running, you can set up Chisel to create a secure SSH tunnel to your server, and then point your local applications to use a local port that is forwarded through the Chisel tunnel to the SOCKS server.

1. On the server, run Chisel with the reverse port forwarding feature, which allows the client to forward the SOCKS server’s port to the local machine:
   ```
   ./chisel server --reverse --port 80
   ```
   
2. On the client, run Chisel with a forwarding command to bind the local port to the server's SOCKS port (assuming SOCKS is on port 1080):
   ```
   chisel client http://yourserver.com R:127.0.0.1:1080:socks
   ```
   
3. Configure your local applications to use the SOCKS proxy at `127.0.0.1` on port `1080`.

### OpenVPN or WireGuard Through Chisel

If you are using OpenVPN or WireGuard, the process is similar, but instead of pointing apps to use a SOCKS proxy, you would use Chisel to forward the appropriate VPN port.

1. Make sure your OpenVPN server or WireGuard is configured and running properly on the server.

2. Run Chisel in server mode on the same or another publicly accessible server. Ensure you have port forwarding (or reverse port forwarding for client-side exposure) enabled if needed.

3. Run Chisel on the client to forward the VPN port. For OpenVPN, it's usually UDP port 1194, and for WireGuard, it's commonly UDP port 51820, though these ports can be configured to be different:
   ```
   chisel client http://yourserver.com 127.0.0.1:1194:1194
   ```
   or for WireGuard:
   ```
   chisel client http://yourserver.com 127.0.0.1:51820:51820
   ```
   
4. Configure your VPN client to connect to `127.0.0.1` at the local forwarded port (1194 for OpenVPN or 51820 for WireGuard).

In this way, Chisel acts as a bridge that encapsulates VPN traffic within an SSH-over-HTTP tunnel. You end up with a connection that traverses through potentially restrictive firewalls since to an outside observer, it appears to be regular HTTP/HTTPS traffic.

Remember that while Chisel can provide a level of indirection and help bypass certain network restrictions, the effectiveness and reliability of such setups can vary based on network configurations and the presence of DPI (Deep Packet Inspection) technologies used by network administrators. Always ensure to comply with network policies and legal requirements of your location when using tunneling software.

What Chisel   difference with
 http proxy then?

Chisel and an HTTP proxy both allow you to forward traffic through a remote server, but they differ significantly in how they operate and what features they offer. Here are some of the key differences:

1. **Protocol Level**:
   - **HTTP Proxy**:
     - Operates at the application layer of the OSI model.
     - Designed to forward HTTP and HTTPS traffic from a client to a destination server.
     - It may support other protocols by using methods like CONNECT for tunneling, but it's mainly intended for web traffic.
   - **Chisel**:
     - Works at the transport layer, effectively creating a tunnel at a lower level in the network stack.
     - Capable of forwarding any TCP or UDP traffic, not just HTTP/HTTPS, making it more versatile for different types of services and protocols.

2. **Encryption and Security**:
   - **HTTP Proxy**:
     - Generally does not provide encryption itself; it relies on the underlying protocols (like HTTPS) for security.
     - When using an HTTP proxy to connect to an HTTPS site, the proxy handles the TCP connection but cannot decrypt the HTTPS traffic.
   - **Chisel**:
     - Establishes an encrypted SSH connection over HTTP, which secures all traffic passing through the tunnel regardless of the protocol being tunneled.
     - Provides a secure way to forward traffic even if the services themselves do not support encryption.

3. **Authentication**:
   - **HTTP Proxy**:
     - May support basic authentication methods, which are not always secure or encrypted.
   - **Chisel**:
     - Uses robust SSH-based authentication, which can include password authentication, public/private key pairs, or even multi-factor authentication if configured on the SSH server.

4. **Use Cases**:
   - **HTTP Proxy**:
     - Often used to cache web content, control internet usage, or circumvent geo-restrictions.
     - Typically used within web browsers or configured for specific applications that support proxy settings.
   - **Chisel**:
     - Ideal for securely connecting to services on a remote network, such as databases, file servers, or internal web applications, often in scenarios like bypassing firewalls or creating secure connections in untrusted environments.

5. **Ease of Configuration and Deployment**:
   - **HTTP Proxy**:
     - Can be simpler to set up for web traffic forwarding and is supported by many web browsers and applications natively.
   - **Chisel**:
     - Might require more technical knowledge to deploy and configure since it involves SSH keys and tunneling concepts.

6. **Additional Features**:
   - **HTTP Proxy**:
     - May include user access controls, content filtering, and web optimization features.
   - **Chisel**:
     - Offers advanced features such as multiple simultaneous tunnel endpoints over a single connection, SOCKS5 proxying, reverse port forwarding, and auto-reconnecting with exponential backoff.

In summary, an HTTP proxy is typically used for web browsing and handling HTTP/S traffic, often without encryption. In contrast, Chisel creates a secure, encrypted tunnel for any TCP/UDP traffic over HTTP, leveraging the SSH protocol, which makes it highly suitable for securely accessing remote network services.
i mean gfw see it as a shh or as a http traffic?

The Great Firewall of China (GFW), known for its advanced filtering and traffic analysis techniques, aims to identify and block traffic that it deems unauthorized or undesirable. Whether the GFW sees traffic as SSH or HTTP when using a tool like Chisel depends on how the tool is being used and configured.

When you use Chisel:

1. **As an HTTP tunnel**:
   - Chisel encapsulates the SSH traffic within HTTP requests and responses. To an external observer, such as a firewall or traffic analyzer like the GFW, this traffic appears as regular HTTP or, if using TLS, HTTPS traffic. Chisel does this by using standard HTTP(S) ports (80 for HTTP and 443 for HTTPS) to establish the connection.

2. **Using standard SSH**:
   - Without additional encapsulation or disguise, SSH traffic has distinct characteristics, uses port 22 by default, and could be fingerprinted by deep packet inspection (DPI) technologies employed by the GFW. This could lead to the SSH traffic being detected and potentially blocked.

Chisel's primary purpose is to tunnel through restrictive firewalls by disguising its traffic as benign HTTP(S) traffic when, in reality, it carries an encrypted SSH payload. By using HTTP as a transport mechanism, Chisel aims to bypass filters that would otherwise detect and block SSH connections.

By default, Chisel is designed to be stealthy, using secured HTTP (HTTPS) for its communications, especially when combined with TLS certificates. This can help avoid detection by the GFW as the traffic will look like regular secure web traffic, making it harder to differentiate from legitimate HTTPS traffic without employing more sophisticated DPI techniques that actively try to identify the content of encrypted connections.

However, it's important to note that no technique is entirely foolproof against a determined and resourceful adversary like the GFW. The GFW is actively adapting to new methods of circumvention, and there have been reports of it using machine learning and other advanced techniques to identify and block various types of proxies and tunnels, including those that use SSH or other methods of encryption and obfuscation.

Users who need to bypass restrictive network controls like the GFW are typically advised to stay updated on the latest technologies and methods for evasion, as the effectiveness of any particular tool or method can vary over time as the GFW updates and alters its filtering and blocking strategies.
encryption vs obfuscation

Encryption and obfuscation are both techniques to protect information, but they serve different purposes and operate in distinct ways. It's crucial to understand their differences, especially when considering security and privacy strategies.

### Encryption

**Purpose:** Encryption is designed to ensure confidentiality and integrity of data. It's a way to securely protect information so that only authorized parties can access it.

**How It Works:**
- Encryption utilizes mathematical algorithms to transform data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext).
- Only entities in possession of the correct key(s) can decrypt the information and revert it to its original form.
- Encryption is underpinned by rigorous mathematical theories and provides a measurable level of security that's hard to break without the key.
- Examples include AES (Advanced Encryption Standard), RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman), and TLS (Transport Layer Security).

**Use Cases:**
- Protecting data transmission over the internet, such as HTTPS.
- Securing sensitive information in databases.
- Encrypted messaging and email communications.
- Protecting files from unauthorized access, both in storage and during transfer.

### Obfuscation

**Purpose:** Obfuscation is designed to make something unclear or difficult to understand. When used in the context of data, it's about making the data ambiguous to those without detailed knowledge of the obfuscation technique used.

**How It Works:**
- Obfuscation employs techniques that hide the true intent or function without altering the fundamental meaning.
- It often relies on changing the appearance of the data or the way the data operates, but not the data itself.
- Unlike encryption, obfuscation doesn't typically offer a mathematical guarantee of security and can often be reversed-engineered or deciphered without possessing a specific key.
- Examples include modifying code structure to prevent readability, data masking, or using VPNs to hide the original IP address.

**Use Cases:**
- Protecting intellectual property by making source code difficult to reverse-engineer.
- Providing a layer of privacy by disguising user activity or data patterns.
- Simplifying compliance with data protection laws by anonymizing personal information.

### Encryption vs. Obfuscation in Practice

**Visibility:**
- Encrypted data appears random and is not human-readable.
- Obfuscated data or code might still be partially readable or recognizable but is intentionally confusing.

**Strength:**
- Encryption typically stands against mathematical and computational attacks, provided that strong algorithms are used and implemented correctly.
- Obfuscation is weaker in terms of security and is often used as a supplementary layer of defense, delaying but not necessarily preventing the reverse engineering of software or the analysis of data.

**Objective:**
- Encryption is about ensuring only authorized access.
- Obfuscation is about delaying unauthorized understanding or discovery.

**In the Context of Firewalls and Censorship:**
- Encrypted traffic using standard protocols like HTTPS can be allowed through firewalls.
- Obfuscated traffic may be used to disguise VPN or tunneling traffic that might not conform to expected patterns and get it past firewalls or deep packet inspectors that are trying to block it.

**Conclusion:**
Encryption is a strong, mathematically sound method for protecting data. Obfuscation is more about adding confusion and complexity to discourage or delay threats but doesn't provide solid security on its own. In many security models, obfuscation is often used in addition to encryption, rather than as a standalone protection method. It's important for organizations and individuals to use encryption first and foremost for security, with obfuscation serving as an additional layer of defense where appropriate.
so gfw can not know that in this http tunnel the ssh encyption mehod is used?

The Great Firewall of China (GFW) uses a variety of sophisticated techniques to inspect and block undesirable traffic, including deep packet inspection (DPI), which can analyze the payload of packets at a granular level. However, whether the GFW can detect SSH encryption within an HTTP tunnel depends on how well the tunnel obfuscates its traffic.

Here's the challenge:

1. **Encrypted Traffic**: Encryption scrambles the contents of a communication, making it unreadable to anyone without the decryption key. If you were to merely use SSH over its standard port (22), the GFW could easily identify and block it because of unique signatures in the packet data that identify the protocol as SSH.

2. **HTTP vs HTTPS**: Regular HTTP traffic is not encrypted, so if you try to tunnel SSH over plain HTTP, the DPI techniques could detect anomalies in the traffic pattern (e.g., binary data where plaintext HTTP data is expected). HTTPS traffic, on the other hand, is encrypted using TLS, making it difficult to directly inspect the payload.

3. **Tunneling with Chisel**: Chisel tunnels SSH over HTTP(S). So, when you set up Chisel with TLS (HTTPS), it looks just like regular secure web traffic, which typically does not raise red flags. This is because the encrypted payload obscures the SSH handshake and session data, making it much more challenging for DPI to determine that SSH is being tunneled through the connection.

4. **Obfuscation**: Beyond encryption, obfuscation adds a layer of protection by disguising the traffic to mask its true nature. Sophisticated tunnels and proxies often use obfuscation techniques to make their traffic look like regular HTTP/HTTPS traffic, thus avoiding automated filtering rules that might detect SSH or VPN protocols.

When you use Chisel or similar tools to encapsulate SSH traffic within HTTPS, you're leveraging both encryption and obfuscation. The GFW sees encryption as used in HTTPS, which is common and generally permitted. Since the actual SSH data is encrypted within the HTTPS stream, and TLS is a standard part of web browsing, this avoids drawing attention. However, this does not mean it's impossible for the GFW to detect such methods, as it continuously evolves to detect evasion techniques, possibly using traffic flow analysis or active probing.

In theory, if the GFW has advanced enough pattern recognition and machine learning capabilities, it may eventually be able to detect anomalies indicative of tunneling, even within encrypted traffic. However, detecting SSH within properly secured and configured HTTPS traffic would be practically challenging due to the robustness of modern encryption standards.

Therefore, while it is much harder for the GFW to detect SSH sessions within an HTTPS tunnel, one must account for the risk of detection due to the sophistication of the GFW's technology and the arms race between censorship and circumvention tools. Users should always be cautious and aware of the legal implications and risks associated with circumventing censorship measures.
so its could be SSH IN TLS ?

Yes, when using Chisel or similar tools that tunnel SSH over HTTPS, you are effectively encapsulating SSH within TLS (Transport Layer Security). Here's how the layered approach works:

1. **SSH**: This is the innermost layer, providing a secure method for creating an encrypted communication channel over an insecure network. SSH includes features like strong encryption, integrity checking, and authentication.

2. **TLS**: This is the outer layer that wraps around SSH when it's being tunneled over HTTPS. TLS secures HTTP connections by providing privacy and data integrity between two or more communicating computer applications (in this case, the Chisel client and Chisel server).

When combined, an SSH session is established first, then it is wrapped inside a TLS session when being transmitted over the network. This process produces a couple of important security benefits:

- **Encryption-in-Encryption**:
  - SSH encrypts the payload (the actual data being sent), making it unreadable to anyone intercepting the packets.
  - TLS then encrypts the entire SSH session, adding an additional layer of encryption. This means that even the fact that SSH is being used is concealed from observers.
  
- **Traffic Obfuscation**:
  - To an observer on the network, such as a firewall or DPI system, the traffic appears to be standard HTTPS web traffic because the TLS encryption masks any distinguishing features of the SSH protocol.
  - This obfuscation makes it difficult for systems designed to filter out or block SSH traffic to identify and stop the connection.

Using SSH within TLS not only doubles down on security by encrypting the data twice but also helps in bypassing filters and firewalls that might block standard SSH traffic. This is especially useful in environments with strict network controls or surveillance, like those behind the GFW.

However, it's worth noting that no method is entirely foolproof against state-level filtering, and entities like the GFW are constantly evolving their technologies to detect and prevent obfuscation and circumvention techniques. Users should proceed with caution and remain informed about the potential risks and legal considerations of using such methods.
is it https or http then?

When you tunnel SSH over Chisel and use TLS, the resulting traffic appears as HTTPS, not HTTP.

**HTTPS (HTTP over TLS):**

- **HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)** is the foundational protocol used by the web to transmit data between a client (like a web browser) and a server. HTTP traffic is unencrypted, which makes it vulnerable to eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks.
- **HTTPS (HTTP Secure)** is essentially HTTP traffic encrypted by **TLS (Transport Layer Security)**. TLS establishes a secure session by performing a handshake between the client and server, ensuring that all data passed between them is encrypted and secure.

When using Chisel with TLS encryption enabled, the SSH traffic is encapsulated within the encrypted HTTPS traffic. This makes it indistinguishable from regular web traffic to passive observers and automated systems designed to analyze network packets, such as the GFW. Thus, it should be referred to as HTTPS rather than HTTP, emphasizing the encryption and secure nature of the connection.

--tls-ca, An optional root certificate bundle used to verify the
    chisel server. Only valid when connecting to the server with
    "https" or "wss". By default, the operating system CAs will be used.

    --tls-skip-verify, Skip server TLS certificate verification of
    chain and host name (if TLS is used for transport connections to
    server). If set, client accepts any TLS certificate presented by
    the server and any host name in that certificate. This only affects
    transport https (wss) connection. Chisel server's public key
    may be still verified (see --fingerprint) after inner connection
    is established.

    --tls-key, a path to a PEM encoded private key used for client 
    authentication (mutual-TLS).

    --tls-cert, a path to a PEM encoded certificate matching the provided 
    private key. The certificate must have client authentication 
    enabled (mutual-TLS).

The options you've listed are related to TLS (Transport Layer Security) configuration for Chisel. They allow you to specify how the Chisel client should handle TLS when communicating with the Chisel server. Let's break down what each option means:

### --tls-ca

- This option allows you to specify a custom root certificate bundle for the Chisel client to use when verifying the Chisel server's TLS certificate. By default, Chisel relies on the trusted certificate authorities (CAs) stored in the operating system to verify the identity of the server during the TLS handshake.
- Using `--tls-ca` is useful if the server is using a self-signed certificate, or a certificate signed by a non-standard CA that is not included in the system's default trusted CA list.
  
### --tls-skip-verify

- When this option is enabled, the Chisel client will not verify the server's TLS certificate. This could leave the connection vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks because the client won't check if the certificate is valid and trusted.
- This option is generally not recommended for production use because it decreases security. It might be used for testing or in controlled environments where the risks are understood and mitigated.
  
### --tls-key

- This option is for specifying the path to a PEM-encoded private key file. This is used when you want to set up mutual TLS authentication (mTLS), where both client and server authenticate each other.
- In a mutual TLS setup, not only does the client verify the server's public certificate, but the server also requires the client to present its own certificate, which must be signed by a CA that the server trusts.
  
### --tls-cert

- Here, you provide the path to a PEM-encoded certificate that matches the private key specified with `--tls-key`. This certificate will be presented to the server for the purpose of client authentication during the mutual TLS handshake.
- The certificate must be configured correctly for client authentication (`mutual-TLS`), meaning it includes the necessary extended key usage (EKU) fields.

**Using TLS with Chisel:**

Implementing these TLS options enhances the security of the Chisel connection by ensuring that data transmitted between the client and the server is encrypted and that both parties can be authenticated.

- For regular TLS, the client checks the server's certificate to create a secure connection.
- For mutual TLS (mTLS), both the client and the server exchange certificates to authenticate each other, creating a bidirectional trust relationship.

It's important to use TLS options correctly to avoid potential security issues. Skipping TLS verification (`--tls-skip-verify`) should only be done with a clear understanding of the risks, while providing custom CA certificates (`--tls-ca`) and setting up mutual TLS (`--tls-key` and `--tls-cert`) are ways to strengthen security where standard trusted CA certificates are insufficient or where enhanced mutual authentication is required.

### Server Setup:

1. Check Open Ports on Your Server:

Connect to your server via SSH and check which ports are open and listening. Ensure port 80 is not being used by another service:

   ssh <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>
   netstat -tulnp

   If another service is using port 80, consider changing its configuration to free up the port.

2. Install Chisel on Your Server:

   Log in to your server and use the following commands to download and set up Chisel:

   mkdir chisel && cd chisel
   wget https://github.com/jpillora/chisel/releases/download/v1.9.1/chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64.gz
   gunzip chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64.gz
   chmod +x chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64


3. Run Chisel in a Persistent Session:

   We will use tmux, a terminal multiplexer, so our Chisel server keeps running even if we disconnect from SSH:

   tmux new -s chisel_session
   ./chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64 server --port 80 --socks5 --proxy http://your-subdomain.yourdomain.com -v

   To detach from the tmux session and leave it running in the background, press Ctrl+B followed by D.

### Client Setup (Windows):

1. Download Chisel Windows Client:
   
   On your Windows machine, download the Chisel client from the GitHub releases page (make sure to choose the correct version for Windows).

2. Setup Chisel Client:

   Extract the downloaded .gz file. To connect to your Chisel server, open Command Prompt in the directory where you’ve extracted Chisel and execute:

   chisel.exe client http://your-subdomain.yourdomain.com 127.0.0.1:5050:socks


### Client Setup (Android):

1. Install Termux and Go Language:

   Download and install Termux from the Play Store. Open Termux and install Go by running the following commands:

   pkg update
   pkg install wget
   pkg install golang


2. Download and Prepare Chisel on Android:

   In Termux, create a new directory for Chisel, download it, and give execution permissions:

   mkdir chisel && cd chisel
   wget https://github.com/jpillora/chisel/releases/download/v1.9.1/chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64.gz
   gunzip chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64.gz
   chmod +x chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64


3. Connect to Chisel Server:

   Run the following command in Termux:

   ./chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64 client http://your-subdomain.yourdomain.com 5050:127.0.0.1:443

$ chisel --help

  Usage: chisel [command] [--help]

  Version: X.Y.Z

  Commands:
    server - runs chisel in server mode
    client - runs chisel in client mode

  Read more:
    https://github.com/jpillora/chisel

$ chisel server --help

  Usage: chisel server [options]

  Options:

    --host, Defines the HTTP listening host – the network interface
    (defaults the environment variable HOST and falls back to 0.0.0.0).

    --port, -p, Defines the HTTP listening port (defaults to the environment
    variable PORT and fallsback to port 8080).

    --key, (deprecated use --keygen and --keyfile instead)
    An optional string to seed the generation of a ECDSA public
    and private key pair. All communications will be secured using this
    key pair. Share the subsequent fingerprint with clients to enable detection
    of man-in-the-middle attacks (defaults to the CHISEL_KEY environment
    variable, otherwise a new key is generate each run).

    --keygen, A path to write a newly generated PEM-encoded SSH private key file.
    If users depend on your --key fingerprint, you may also include your --key to
    output your existing key. Use - (dash) to output the generated key to stdout.

    --keyfile, An optional path to a PEM-encoded SSH private key. When
    this flag is set, the --key option is ignored, and the provided private key
    is used to secure all communications. (defaults to the CHISEL_KEY_FILE
    environment variable). Since ECDSA keys are short, you may also set keyfile
    to an inline base64 private key (e.g. chisel server --keygen - | base64).

    --authfile, An optional path to a users.json file. This file should
    be an object with users defined like:
      {
        "<user:pass>": ["<addr-regex>","<addr-regex>"]
      }
    when <user> connects, their <pass> will be verified and then
    each of the remote addresses will be compared against the list
    of address regular expressions for a match. Addresses will
    always come in the form "<remote-host>:<remote-port>" for normal remotes
    and "R:<local-interface>:<local-port>" for reverse port forwarding
    remotes. This file will be automatically reloaded on change.

    --auth, An optional string representing a single user with full
    access, in the form of <user:pass>. It is equivalent to creating an
    authfile with {"<user:pass>": [""]}. If unset, it will use the
    environment variable AUTH.

    --keepalive, An optional keepalive interval. Since the underlying
    transport is HTTP, in many instances we'll be traversing through
    proxies, often these proxies will close idle connections. You must
    specify a time with a unit, for example '5s' or '2m'. Defaults
    to '25s' (set to 0s to disable).

    --backend, Specifies another HTTP server to proxy requests to when
    chisel receives a normal HTTP request. Useful for hiding chisel in
    plain sight.

    --socks5, Allow clients to access the internal SOCKS5 proxy. See
    chisel client --help for more information.

    --reverse, Allow clients to specify reverse port forwarding remotes
    in addition to normal remotes.

    --tls-key, Enables TLS and provides optional path to a PEM-encoded
    TLS private key. When this flag is set, you must also set --tls-cert,
    and you cannot set --tls-domain.

    --tls-cert, Enables TLS and provides optional path to a PEM-encoded
    TLS certificate. When this flag is set, you must also set --tls-key,
    and you cannot set --tls-domain.

    --tls-domain, Enables TLS and automatically acquires a TLS key and
    certificate using LetsEncrypt. Setting --tls-domain requires port 443.
    You may specify multiple --tls-domain flags to serve multiple domains.
    The resulting files are cached in the "$HOME/.cache/chisel" directory.
    You can modify this path by setting the CHISEL_LE_CACHE variable,
    or disable caching by setting this variable to "-". You can optionally
    provide a certificate notification email by setting CHISEL_LE_EMAIL.

    --tls-ca, a path to a PEM encoded CA certificate bundle or a directory
    holding multiple PEM encode CA certificate bundle files, which is used to 
    validate client connections. The provided CA certificates will be used 
    instead of the system roots. This is commonly used to implement mutual-TLS. 

    --pid Generate pid file in current working directory

    -v, Enable verbose logging

    --help, This help text

  Signals:
    The chisel process is listening for:
      a SIGUSR2 to print process stats, and
      a SIGHUP to short-circuit the client reconnect timer

  Version:
    X.Y.Z

  Read more:
    https://github.com/jpillora/chisel

To improve the setup to ensure SSH traffic is tunneled over TLS, you need to adjust the configurations for both the Chisel server and client to enable and properly use TLS. Below are the enhanced setup instructions, which include the necessary TLS configurations:

### Server Setup:

1. **Check Open Ports on Your Server:**

   SSH into your server and check for open ports. Ensure port 443 (the default port for HTTPS) is available.

   ```
   ssh <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>
   netstat -tulnp | grep :443
   ```

   If port 443 is used by another service and you don't intend to replace it, you can use another port and include the port number in the client configuration.

2. **Install Chisel on Your Server:**

   ```
   mkdir chisel && cd chisel
   wget https://github.com/jpillora/chisel/releases/download/v1.9.1/chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64.gz
   gunzip chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64.gz
   chmod +x chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64
   ```

3. **Setup TLS Certificates:**

   Generate TLS certificates or obtain them from a certificate authority, such as Let's Encrypt.

   ```
   # If using Let's Encrypt (replace <your-domain.com> with your actual domain)
   sudo apt-get update
   sudo apt-get install certbot
   sudo certbot certonly --standalone -d <your-domain.com>
   
   # Certificate and key will be in /etc/letsencrypt/live/<your-domain.com>/
   ```

4. **Run Chisel in a Persistent Session with TLS:**

   Use `tmux` to run the Chisel server with TLS options, specifying paths to your TLS certificate (`--tls-cert`) and private key (`--tls-key`).

   ```
   tmux new -s chisel_session
   ./chisel_1.9.1_linux_amd64 server --port 443 --tls-key /path/to/privkey.pem --tls-cert /path/to/fullchain.pem --socks5 --reverse -v
   ```
   
   Press `Ctrl+B` then `D` to detach from the tmux session.

### Client Setup (Windows):

1. **Download Chisel Windows Client:**

2. **Setup Chisel Client with TLS Verification:**

   Assuming you have a valid certificate from a recognized CA, you can use the chisel client without the `--tls-ca` option (as the server's certificate will verify against system CAs).

   ```
   chisel.exe client https://<your-domain.com> 127.0.0.1:5050:socks
   ```

   If you used a self-signed certificate or a certificate from a non-standard CA on the server, you will need to specify the CA's certificate with the `--tls-ca` flag for the client to verify it.

### Client Setup (Android):

1. **Install Termux and Download Chisel:**

2. **Connect to Chisel Server with TLS Verification:**

   Similarly, if your certificate is from a well-known CA, there's no need for `--tls-ca`.

   ```
   ./chisel_1.9.1_linux_arm64 client https://<your-domain.com> 127.0.0.1:5050:socks
   ```

   If you are using a non-standard CA or self-signed certificate, then you must specify the `--tls-ca` option with the path to the CA's certificate.

By running the server on port 443 with TLS options, you're ensuring that Chisel creates a secure tunnel using TLS, which makes the SSH traffic tunneled within it appear as regular HTTPS traffic to outside observers, including firewalls and network filters.

Make sure to replace placeholder paths, domain names, and port numbers with those specific to your situation. Furthermore, all clients connecting to the server will need to trust the server's TLS certificate, either by using a certificate from a standard CA or by specifying a custom CA with `--tls-ca`.
